topic: Part 6.14 κ Coupling Cluster — A Cross-Eclipse Vise (c3, deep-attack post-c2)
trigger_note: Queue item 334 (pushed_by=decider 2026-05-11T13:24:31Z, class=deep-attack) explicitly asks c3 to (1) verify ISS-1963/1967 patches landed, (2) attack the 6 new c2 holes (ISS-1972..1977), (3) re-examine Chebychev argument engagement. This is the post-c2 fresh adversarial pass; the c2 review under queue_id 333 was popped at 2026-05-11T13:24:04Z and is filed under reviews/SEC-6.14-kappa-cluster.c2.json.
our_argument_summary: Section 6.14 builds a structural cross-eclipse vise around κ = 1.677 nT/µGal: either κ applies generally — in which case the 1999 European and 2009 East Asian eclipses' magnetic anomalies imply few-µGal SG pulses that Membach 1999 (Van Camp 2001) and the Chinese 2009 SG network failed to detect — or κ has unpublished scope conditions that exempt those eclipses but also destroy PRED-TIER-3's 10–13 µGal August 2026 derivation. The structural argument remains correct; the verdict (both horns lose at least one WIN/PRED) is defensible. Two of the c1 carry-forwards have been patched between c2 and c3: ISS-1963 (Domain #7 → #5; verified: current sections.json text reads 'monitor.py Domain #5 hardcodes KAPPA = 1.67') and ISS-1967 (1,000× → 50–500×; verified: current text reads 'at roughly 50–500× the precision of the Mohe spring gravimeter'). NEITHER of the other c1 carry-forwards has been patched: ISS-1964 (Crossley year/DOI mismatch — still linked to 10.1007/s00024-022-03000-4 with in-text '2013'), ISS-1965 (Chebychev still bundled in 'post-hoc rescues' parenthetical with bedrock/noise/parameter-shift — no empirical engagement), ISS-1966 (storm-null citation still uncited; no Boy & Lyard or Imanishi reference added). NONE of the c2 holes has been patched: ISS-1972 (2009 ΔB still ensemble-inferred from Tomás & Mendoza-Torres 2018, no specific Chinese-station measurement); ISS-1973 (Sun et al. 2010 still load-bearing-and-unlocatable, doi:10.6038/cjg20130306 still the parenthetical substitute on a different topic); ISS-1974 (Van Camp 2001 methodology still uncharacterized); ISS-1975 (PRED-TIER-3 'derives ... by dividing ΔB by κ' still stated as fact, not inference); ISS-1976 (Hvoždara & Prigancová still linked via researchgate.net); ISS-1977 (30–600 factor span still selectively pairs predicted-pulse range with favorable noise band). c3 adds 6 NEW holes (3 moderate, 3 minor): the 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' naming as published scope hint that the section dismisses without acknowledging; the cross-reference paragraph's '207 magnetic measurements, each implying a co-located SG prediction through κ' which overstates co-location given sparse global SG network; an internal self-consistency issue between H6 reframe and the 'no published mechanism' claim that follows it; absence of temporal-resolution characterization for the SG residuals (distinct from frequency-filter question — this is about binning); the '(A note on units)' parenthetical that reads as a process artifact leaking into public review; and the WIN-058b r=0.992 Monte Carlo refutation being internal-only (no peer-reviewed citation), which crossrefs back into this section. None of the new c3 findings shifts the verdict; all are minor_edit class. Confidence drops from c2's 0.78 to 0.74 because the carry-forward count is now 9 unresolved over 3 cycles — citation hygiene is the standing weakness even though the structural argument is firm.

relevant_files: data/sections.json:part6 (current text verified this run via git pull --rebase post-879691a)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: κ = 1.677 nT/µGal published in dome live-power dashboard Domain 5 (NOT 7 — verified ISS-1963 patched in c2→c3 window; current sections.json reads 'monitor.py Domain #5 hardcodes KAPPA = 1.67'). PRED-TIER-3 entry in data/predictions.json reads claim='SG contact-phase κ test, 10–13 µGal in Level-3 residuals', test_window='August 2026', test_window_closes='2026-08-12', our_verdict='pending' — the dome's published target is 10–13 µGal but the entry does NOT explicitly state the derivation 'predicted ΔB / κ' anywhere c3 could find (ISS-1975 confirmed unfixed). WIN-058b detail_evidence (verified c3) contains the Monte Carlo argument 'τ = 30 s, N = 1,620 samples matching the Exhibit A time window' that the section's storm-corollary subsection cross-references as 'as the WIN-058b detail already documents' — this Monte Carlo is INTERNAL (our own simulation), not peer-reviewed (new c3 H14 below).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Live-power dashboard Domain 5 still presents κ as a hardcoded predicted/observed pair (1.67/1.677, 5.0% tolerance, PASS). The 'validation' is recomputing 10.9 / 6.5 = 1.677 from the same hardcoded inputs — display, not check. Section now correctly characterizes Domain as #5 (was #7 in c1/c2 era).
data_sources_used: Mohe 1997 spring gravimeter (claimed via Wang et al. 2000)
Tomás & Mendoza-Torres 2018 (ensemble inference for 2009 ΔB — see ISS-1972)
Sun et al. 2010 (claimed, unlocatable — see ISS-1973)
Van Camp et al. 2001 (1999 SG null, methodology uncharacterized — see ISS-1974)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Dome offers no published scope condition limiting κ to specific eclipse magnitudes, station latitudes, coverage thresholds, or bedrock types. Section accurately notes this absence is what closes the vise. The section's 'What the dome could do' subsection lists four carve-outs (Chebychev low-pass, Devonian carbonate vs basement granite bedrock, instrumental noise, parameter shift) and dismisses ALL FOUR collectively as 'post-hoc rescues constructed after the data refused to cooperate' — no empirical engagement with the Chebychev counter specifically, which is the dome's strongest (ISS-1965 confirmed unfixed at c3). Note: WIN-058b is titled 'Bedrock Induction Ratio κ = 1.677, PRED-KAPPA-001' — the 'Bedrock Induction' naming is a published-prior scope hint that the section's dismissal underweights (new c3 H9 below).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: ISS-1963 (Domain #7 → #5) is PATCHED in current sections.json. The c1/c2 'monitor.py Domain #7' factual error is now resolved at all three integration sites (SEC-6.14, WIN-013, WIN-014) per closed-issues.json. ISS-1967 (1,000× precision) is also PATCHED: current text reads '50–500×' (within the c1-recommended range '~50–500× or two orders of magnitude'). No remaining code-claim discrepancies in the section.

code_analysis_tags_note: code_analysis_tags do not apply to section-level reviews (per-WIN tags only). Section 6.14's per-WIN tags remain valid: WIN-012 derives_from_dome=false (κ is fitted, not derived from dome geometry); WIN-058b post_hoc=true (κ recomputed after storm data published); WIN-068 reviewed=true (39-eclipse ensemble Std Model Explains).

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c1 (ISS-1964), UNFIXED at c3. 'What past SG data actually shows' subsection links 'Crossley et al. (2013) and the J9-Strasbourg intercomparison' to https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00024-022-03000-4. The year-in-DOI (2022) does not match the year-in-citation-text (2013). Verified this c3 run: DOI still linked, in-text '2013' still present. A defender will pull the 2022 paper at that DOI and either find content inconsistent with 'modern SG residual noise floors of 0.1–1 µGal at the relevant timescales' OR find the 0.1–1 µGal characterization but flag the year mismatch as sloppy citation. Three cycles outstanding now; analyst-assigned per open-issues.json but no patch landed.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (i) change in-text year to 2022 if the linked DOI is correct, or (ii) find the correct 2013 DOI and replace the link. The Crossley, Hinderer & Riccardi (2013) 'The measurement of surface gravity' citation is Rep. Prog. Phys. 76 046101 (DOI 10.1088/0034-4885/76/4/046101) — different paper than the 2022 one currently linked. Verify which paper substantiates the 0.1–1 µGal noise-floor claim before patching.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c1 (ISS-1965), UNFIXED at c3. The 'What the dome could do' subsection still lumps four distinct dome rebuttals — Chebychev low-pass, Devonian-vs-basement bedrock, instrumental noise, parameter-shift — into a single parenthetical list and dismisses all four collectively as 'post-hoc rescues constructed after the data refused to cooperate.' The Chebychev rebuttal is the dome's STRONGEST counter: if Level-3 SG reduction pipelines low-pass filter contact-phase transients by construction (a real, well-documented feature of standard SG data processing — see Boy & Hinderer 2006 on SG tidal residual pipelines), the predicted κ-driven pulse would not appear in Level-3 residuals regardless of whether the coupling is real. Three cycles outstanding. Dismissing the Chebychev argument as 'post-hoc' is rhetorical, not empirical. A defender will say: 'you are calling a standard methodological feature post-hoc because it inconveniences your refutation; show the filter cutoffs and demonstrate that contact-phase frequencies survive the pipeline.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add one dedicated paragraph distinguishing Chebychev (a real methodological feature of Level-3 reduction) from the bedrock/noise/parameter-shift carve-outs (which lack independent grounding). Counter-engagement: (i) cite the actual filter cutoff used in Van Camp 2001 or the Crossley intercomparison literature; (ii) note that raw pre-filter residuals are publicly archived at the Global Geodynamics Project (http://www.eas.slu.edu/GGP/) — if κ predicts a 3-9 µGal pulse, the raw Membach 1999 record SHOULD show it pre-reduction; (iii) if no such pulse appears even in raw data, the Chebychev counter fails on its own grounds. Engage on the empirical merits rather than rhetorical dismissal.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c1 (ISS-1966), UNFIXED at c3. 'Storm corollary (WIN-058b)' subsection still asserts 'the published per-storm SG residual literature does not show systematic gravity pulses tracking storm magnetic perturbations through κ' and 'dedicated SG-storm coupling searches have not found them' without citing a single specific survey paper. Boy & Lyard 2008 (J. Geodyn., on SG tidal residual hygiene) and Imanishi et al. 2004 (Earth, Planets, and Space, on SG response to atmospheric/geomagnetic forcing) are the plausible existing-literature anchors c1 originally suggested; neither has been added in three cycles. A defender either disputes the claim ('cite your search') or accepts it but argues the published literature was not specifically looking for κ-style signals.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (i) cite Boy & Lyard 2008 and/or Imanishi et al. 2004 (verify the storm-coupling content is there before citing), or (ii) soften to 'we are not aware of published SG-storm-coupling studies reporting systematic gravity pulses; the WIN-058b detail page documents this absence in more depth' — internal-cross-reference is at least transparent about the limit of citation.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c2 (ISS-1972), UNFIXED at c3. 'What κ predicts during past eclipses' subsection still infers 2009 East Asian eclipse magnetic perturbations from a global ensemble (Tomás & Mendoza-Torres 2018, '5–15 nT for high-coverage events') without citing a specific 2009 measurement at a named Chinese station. Two cycles outstanding now. The 2009 July 22 eclipse was instrumented by Chinese INTERMAGNET-equivalent stations (Hangzhou/HZT, Changchun/CHC, Beijing/BMT plus others) and specific ΔB measurements exist in the Chinese geophysical literature (Xu et al., Chinese J. Geophys. ~2010 vintage, would be a search-start). A defender will argue the 2009 leg of the cross-eclipse vise rests on ensemble-inference rather than measured ΔB, weakening it relative to the 1999 leg (which cites Strestík/Hvoždara/Curto specifically).
holes_found.recommendation: Find at least one specific 2009 East Asian eclipse magnetic measurement at a Chinese station (HZT, CHC, or BMT). If 5–15 nT holds, cite the specific measurement; if actual 2009 ΔB at those stations was smaller, κ-predicted pulse magnitude drops accordingly and the SG null margin narrows but the structural argument still works. Either way, replace ensemble-inference with measured ΔB for 2009 — the 1999 case already does this; 2009 should too.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c2 (ISS-1973), UNFIXED at c3. The 2009 East Asian SG-null claim is still single-load-bearing on 'Sun et al. 2010' (the dome's own citation, which the section honestly admits cannot be located: 'closest verified peer-reviewed Chinese-language record being doi:10.6038/cjg20130306, 2013'). The substitute 10.6038/cjg20130306 is on the Mohe 1997 reanalysis lineage, NOT a 2009 SG survey. The 2009 SG-null therefore rests on the dome's own (uncitable) Sun 2010 reference with no independent corroborating literature in the section. A defender will object: 'you refute κ via a null sourced from the very dome paper you're refuting — that's circular.' Two cycles outstanding; analyst-assigned per open-issues.json but no patch landed.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (i) locate the actual Sun et al. 2010 reference (likely Sun Hong-Ping et al., possibly in Earth, Planets and Space or Acta Geodaetica et Geophysica Hungarica 2010) and cite it directly; or (ii) find an independent 2009 East Asian SG study not sourced from the dome — candidates: Crossley/Hinderer/Calvo 2012 EGU summary; IGGCAS Chinese SG network publications; ICET Bulletin (BIM) 2009-vintage residuals. If no independent source is locatable, soften the 2009 case from 'reported no eclipse-coincident gravity perturbation' to 'no peer-reviewed report of an eclipse-coincident SG pulse from the 2009 campaign has been located despite the network's continuous operation' — acknowledges absence-of-evidence vs evidence-of-absence distinction.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c2 (ISS-1974), UNFIXED at c3. The Van Camp et al. 2001 citation (DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637) is still characterized as 'reported no eclipse-related gravity signal above the instrument noise floor of order 0.01–0.1 µGal at minute timescales' without describing the data-reduction methodology. If Van Camp 2001 applied (i) standard tidal model corrections (DDW or Wahr-Dehant), (ii) atmospheric pressure admittance correction (~−0.3 µGal/mbar), (iii) drift removal, and (iv) any low-pass smoothing, then the final 'residual' the paper reports as null may EXCLUDE the frequency band where κ-predicted contact-phase transients would appear. This gives the dome's Chebychev counter (Hole 1 above) empirical anchorage in the very paper we cite for the null. A defender who reads Van Camp 2001 carefully may find the null was constructed in a way that mathematically guaranteed the absence of the signal κ predicts — not because κ is wrong, but because the data pipeline filtered out the relevant band. Two cycles outstanding.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence characterizing Van Camp 2001's data-reduction pipeline. Two acceptable forms: (i) 'Van Camp 2001 applied tidal and atmospheric corrections at sub-daily timescales but retained minute-resolution residuals across C1 and C4, where any κ-predicted contact-phase pulse of a few µGal would appear above the 0.01–0.1 µGal noise floor' — verify this characterization is accurate before publishing; OR (ii) acknowledge the methodological caveat: 'Van Camp 2001's reported null is at minute-to-hour timescales after standard tidal and atmospheric corrections; if the dome argues κ predicts a transient narrower than this band, the SG null does not directly refute it — see What the dome could do.' Both eliminate the unaddressed methodology objection.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c2 (ISS-1975), UNFIXED at c3. 'What κ is and why the dome cannot abandon it' subsection still asserts 'PRED-TIER-3 — the dome's headline forward-looking prediction for the August 2026 total solar eclipse — derives its 10–13 µGal SG-pulse target by dividing the predicted ΔB at named stations by κ.' Verified this c3 run by re-reading data/predictions.json:PRED-TIER-3: the dome's published entry reads claim='SG contact-phase κ test, 10–13 µGal in Level-3 residuals' and does NOT explicitly state the 'ΔB / κ' derivation pathway. The natural reading is that the 10–13 µGal IS derived this way, but the section currently states this as fact rather than inference. A defender will say: 'the dome did not publish that derivation; you invented it to make the vise close.' If the dome's 10–13 µGal target came from some other derivation (e.g., direct dome-geometry calculation, or Mohe scaling × some factor), the section's claim that PRED-TIER-3 'has no published mechanism producing the 10–13 µGal target' without κ is wrong. Two cycles outstanding.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (i) locate dome-published material that explicitly derives PRED-TIER-3's 10–13 µGal via 'ΔB / κ' — check raw-text/03-predictions.txt, raw-text/05-model.txt, the dome's predictions.html appendix, monitor.py — and cite the source; OR (ii) reframe the section's claim as inference: 'PRED-TIER-3 cites no derivation pathway in its published entry; the natural reading is that 10–13 µGal = (predicted ΔB at named stations) / κ; absent any alternative dome-published derivation, the κ-cluster argument applies, and the dome would need to publish a non-κ derivation to rebut.' Reframe preserves structural argument while removing the unattributed-derivation vulnerability.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c2 (ISS-1976), UNFIXED at c3. 'What κ predicts during past eclipses' subsection still links Hvoždara & Prigancová (2002) via researchgate.net (https://www.researchgate.net/publication/29623808_Solar_eclipse_effect_on_geomagnetic_induction_parameters), not a primary DOI. ResearchGate hosts author-uploaded preprints that can rot, be removed, or differ from the published version. The cited figures (ΔX = −5 to −8 nT, ΔY = +12 to +15 nT) require primary-source verification — the canonical citation for Hvoždara & Prigancová 2002 is likely in Studia Geophysica et Geodaetica (Springer) or a similar venue. Two cycles outstanding.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the ResearchGate link with the primary publication DOI. Verify the figures attributed (ΔX = −5 to −8 nT, ΔY = +12 to +15 nT) against the primary publication. Also verify Strestík (2001) figures (ΔY ≈ +10 nT, ΔX ≈ −5 nT) against the Springer chapter — c2 left this unchecked.

holes_found.description: CARRY-FORWARD FROM c2 (ISS-1977), UNFIXED at c3. 'What past SG data actually shows' subsection still states 'A few-µGal predicted pulse exceeds the SG noise floor by factors of 30–600.' The 30× lower bound implicitly uses ~3 µGal / 0.1 µGal; the 600× upper bound uses ~6 µGal / 0.01 µGal. But the predicted-pulse range elsewhere in the section is 3–9 µGal (3–6 ΔX-only, 6–9 combined), and the two noise-floor bands in the section are 0.01–0.1 µGal (Van Camp 2001) and 0.1–1 µGal (Crossley 2013). Using the Crossley/J9 0.1–1 µGal band, the factor over noise drops to 3× to 90×. The '30–600' selectively pairs predicted-pulse range with the more favorable Van Camp noise band. Two cycles outstanding.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten to two bands explicitly: 'using Van Camp 2001's instrumental noise floor of 0.01–0.1 µGal, the predicted 3–9 µGal pulse exceeds noise by factors of 30–900; using Crossley 2013's post-reduction residual floor of 0.1–1 µGal, the margin narrows to 3–90×. Either way, multi-instrument continuous sampling at both 1999 and 2009 would have detected the signal.' Acknowledging both bands disarms the inconsistency objection while preserving the conclusion.

holes_found.description: NEW IN c3. The dome publishes κ as the 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' under WIN-058b's full label ('Bedrock Induction Ratio κ = 1.677, PRED-KAPPA-001'; verified this c3 by reading data/wins.json WIN-058b.claim). The name 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' is a published-prior scope hint: it suggests the dome considers κ to be specifically about EM-induced gravity-mimicking signals in conductive bedrock during high-EM-flux events, not a universal coupling that should hold at every eclipse and every storm regardless of substrate. The section's 'What the dome could do' subsection lists 'Membach's Devonian carbonate bedrock differs from Mohe's basement granite' as ONE of four dismissed carve-outs, but does not engage with the fact that the dome's OWN NAMING is a published-prior signal that bedrock conditions matter. A defender will say: 'we named it Bedrock Induction Ratio because we always intended it as a bedrock-conditional coupling; your cross-eclipse vise applies κ outside its published scope and then refutes the misapplication.' The section currently treats this as a post-hoc carve-out when in fact the bedrock-conditionality is in the title of the WIN being applied. This is the strongest principled escape the dome has, and the section doesn't acknowledge it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence acknowledging the 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' naming as a published-prior scope hint, then defuse: 'The dome labels κ the Bedrock Induction Ratio, which could be read as a bedrock-conditional coupling. But the dome publishes no quantitative bedrock conductivity threshold above or below which the coupling applies; in the absence of a published threshold, the constant κ = 1.677 is applied identically across the live-power dashboard, WIN-012/013/014, and PRED-TIER-3. Treating κ as bedrock-conditional retroactively to exempt 1999/2009 requires the dome to publish, in advance of August 2026, the specific bedrock conductivity values that gate the coupling — and to publish them in a timestamped venue independent of the dome's mutable repository (see Timestamp Error tab).' This co-opts the dome's naming as a scope-condition request rather than dismissing it.

holes_found.description: NEW IN c3. 'Cross-reference to the WIN registry' subsection states 'WIN-068 (39-eclipse magnetic ensemble 1991–2016, Std Model Explains — research candidate for ensemble-scale κ refutation, 207 magnetic measurements, each implying a co-located SG prediction through κ).' The clause 'each implying a co-located SG prediction through κ' overstates SG co-location. The global superconducting gravimeter network has roughly 12–15 long-running stations primarily clustered in Europe (Membach, Strasbourg J9, Wettzell, Black Forest Observatory, Conrad/Pecny), North America (Boulder, Geneva, Cantley), East Asia (Beijing, Wuhan, Lhasa, Kyoto), Australia (Canberra, Mt Stromlo), Antarctica (Syowa, Concordia), and a handful elsewhere. The 39 eclipses in WIN-068's ensemble span global tracks including the South Pacific, Indian Ocean, Southern Ocean, Saharan, and Arctic paths where there are NO SG instruments anywhere near totality. Plausibly 5–10 of the 39 eclipses had a continuously-sampling SG within useful distance of the path of totality, not 207. The phrase '207 magnetic measurements, each implying a co-located SG prediction' implies one SG test per measurement, which is empirically false. A sophisticated defender pulls up the global SG site map (Global Geodynamics Project archive) and the WIN-068 eclipse path catalogue, intersects them, and demonstrates the actual number is small — undermining the 'ensemble-scale κ refutation' framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the overstated 207-each-SG-prediction claim with the accurate scope: 'WIN-068 (39-eclipse magnetic ensemble 1991–2016, Std Model Explains — research candidate for the subset of eclipses where SGs were continuously sampling near totality, plausibly 5–10 events; each such case implies an additional κ-refutation test).' Optional follow-up: identify the subset by intersecting the WIN-068 path catalogue with the GGP SG site/operational-date catalogue, and list the candidate co-located events explicitly in WIN-068's detail.

holes_found.description: NEW IN c3. Self-consistency between the section's H6 (ISS-1975) and the SENTENCE THAT FOLLOWS IT in 'What κ is and why the dome cannot abandon it' subsection. The current text reads: '... PRED-TIER-3 ... derives its 10–13 µGal SG-pulse target by dividing the predicted ΔB at named stations by κ. Without κ as a real coupling, PRED-TIER-3 has no published mechanism producing the 10–13 µGal target.' If H6/ISS-1975 lands as a reframe (the section acknowledges the ΔB/κ derivation is OUR inference, not dome-published), the second sentence — 'has no published mechanism producing the 10–13 µGal target' — overstates equally. The two sentences carry the same epistemic weight: if (a) the derivation is inferred, then (b) the absence-of-alternative-derivation is also inferred. The H6 patch should land both sentences together as inference, or neither: 'PRED-TIER-3 cites no derivation pathway in its published entry. The natural reading is that 10–13 µGal = (predicted ΔB at named stations) / κ, and we have located no alternative derivation in the dome's published material; absent such an alternative, κ owns the derivation and the cross-eclipse vise applies.' This preserves the structural conclusion while consistently labeling both halves as inference.
holes_found.recommendation: When patching ISS-1975, patch the FOLLOWING sentence at the same time — they share epistemic weight. Suggested combined replacement: 'PRED-TIER-3 cites no derivation pathway in its published entry; the natural reading is that 10–13 µGal = (predicted ΔB at named stations) / κ, and we have not located any alternative derivation in the dome's published material. Absent such an alternative, κ owns the derivation. The dome cannot drop κ without dropping its strongest live discriminator — or, alternatively, publishing in advance of August 2026 a non-κ derivation for the 10–13 µGal target.' Decider should treat H6 and this new H11 as a single coupled patch.

holes_found.description: NEW IN c3. 'What past SG data actually shows' subsection says SGs were 'continuously sampled across C1 and C4' (Membach 1999) and 'sampling continuously at 0.1–1 µGal post-reduction precision' (China 2009) but does not specify the TEMPORAL RESOLUTION of the published residuals. SG instruments raw-sample internally at 1–2 Hz, but standard Level-3 published residuals are typically decimated to 1-minute or 1-hour bins after tidal/atmospheric reduction. If the predicted κ-driven contact-phase pulse is sub-minute (eclipse contact phases C1 and C4 are instantaneous transitions, and any associated transient could plausibly be tens of seconds wide — narrower than the standard 1-min bin), the published 1-min Level-3 residuals may not have the temporal resolution to capture it even before considering frequency filtering. This is distinct from ISS-1974 (which is about whether the frequency band is filtered): this is about temporal binning. A defender can argue: 'your null is at 1-min temporal resolution; κ predicts a 30-second pulse; you're not comparing like-for-like even before considering filter cutoffs.' Worse, the section already cites '0.01–0.1 µGal at minute timescales' (Van Camp 2001) and '0.1–1 µGal at the relevant timescales' (Crossley 2013) — both are explicitly per-minute figures, which CONFIRMS the published residuals are at minute resolution and implicitly acknowledges the temporal-resolution caveat that the section then does not address.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence characterizing the temporal resolution: 'Both Van Camp 2001 and the published Chinese 2009 residuals report SG noise at minute timescales (the standard Level-3 binning). A κ-predicted pulse of width 1 minute or longer would appear in these residuals; a sub-minute transient (e.g., 30-second contact-phase pulse) would be diluted by the 1-min binning by roughly a factor of 2 amplitude, reducing the 3–9 µGal predicted pulse to 1.5–4.5 µGal observed — still well above the 0.01–1 µGal noise floor.' If true, this neutralizes the temporal-resolution counter; if false (e.g., if the pulse is short enough to be diluted below noise), the section needs to acknowledge a methodological caveat. Either way, address the binning question explicitly.

holes_found.description: NEW IN c3. The current section contains a parenthetical paragraph reading: '(A note on units: the dome publishes κ as a coupling with ΔB in the numerator and Δg in the denominator. To produce a predicted Δg from a measured ΔB, one divides — Δg = ΔB / κ. The HNOTE that commissioned this section uses "κ × ΔB" as a coupling-loose shorthand; the substantive argument is identical, and the µGal figures cited above are correctly computed via division.)' This reads as a process artifact that leaked into the public review — the section is publicly explaining to itself that an internal commissioning note used loose shorthand. A defender will see this and conclude 'the reviewers themselves were confused enough about κ's direction that they had to publish a clarification mid-section.' The inline math (Δg = ΔB / κ) is already correct everywhere in the section; the parenthetical adds no information for the reader and damages credibility by exposing internal process. Easy cosmetic fix.
holes_found.recommendation: Delete the entire '(A note on units...)' parenthetical paragraph. The inline derivation 'Δg = ΔB/κ ≈ 3 to 9 µGal' is self-explanatory and correct as published; the reader does not need to be told that an internal note used different shorthand. Replace with nothing; the surrounding paragraphs flow correctly without it.

holes_found.description: NEW IN c3. 'The storm corollary (WIN-058b)' subsection states 'Monte Carlo at physically realistic SNR produces r > 0.96 from shared forcing alone, as the WIN-058b detail already documents.' Verified this c3 by reading data/wins.json WIN-058b.detail_evidence: the Monte Carlo IS documented there ('Monte Carlo simulation of two independent sensors responding to a shared storm-envelope driver with autocorrelated noise (τ = 30 s, N = 1,620 samples matching the Exhibit A time window)'). The Monte Carlo is OURS — an internal simulation generated by the review pipeline — not a citation to peer-reviewed methodological literature on correlated-noise simulation. A defender will object: 'you dismiss the r = 0.992 correlation by citing your own simulation as evidence.' WIN-058b's verdict ('Not Demonstrated' rather than 'Refuted by Data') already partly acknowledges this — the Monte Carlo is suggestive, not conclusive. But the cross-reference in SEC-6.14 is framed more assertively ('the existing detail prose'), inheriting more confidence from the storm-corollary subsection than the cross-referenced detail actually carries. The section quietly leans on an internal simulation to dismiss a load-bearing storm-side correlation.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (i) cite peer-reviewed methodological work on Monte Carlo correlation from shared forcing (e.g., Roberts & Ruvinsky 1990 on cross-spectral analysis under shared driver; or standard textbook treatment in Bendat & Piersol 'Random Data: Analysis and Measurement Procedures') in the WIN-058b detail and update the SEC-6.14 cross-reference to inherit the upgraded citation; OR (ii) hedge the SEC-6.14 cross-reference to match WIN-058b's actual epistemic status: 'the r = 0.992 temporal correlation cited as confirmation in WIN-058b's prose is not, on its own, evidence for a coupling — it is consistent with shared electromagnetic forcing under a wide range of physically realistic SNR conditions, as the WIN-058b detail simulation documents.' The hedge is honest about what the Monte Carlo establishes (consistency with the null, not refutation of the alternative).

stronger_arguments.description: (Carry from c2; still applicable.) The strongest sharpening of the κ vise would be a co-located ΔB and Δg measurement from the SAME eclipse, demonstrating that the dome's own coupling ratio (10.9 nT / 6.5 µGal = 1.677) is reproduced (or not) within a single event. The 2017 North American eclipse path crossed several Earthscope Transportable Array stations and INTERMAGNET observatories simultaneously. If any of those was co-located with an SG (Black Forest Observatory or similar), the BOU magnetic measurement that gave us 10.9 nT has a co-located gravity measurement; if that SG showed the 6.5 µGal pulse the κ derivation requires, κ is confirmed within a single eclipse. If it did NOT (the predicted outcome given Van Camp 2001 + Crossley 2013), the dome's κ-derivation FAILS WITHIN ITS OWN NUMERATOR EVENT — a much sharper refutation than the cross-eclipse vise, which still allows defenders the 'different events, different scope' escape.
sources: Black Forest Observatory SG Aug 2017 (BFO archive, https://www.gik.kit.edu/bfo.php)
Earthscope Transportable Array gravity stations (USArray gravity, https://www.usarray.org/)

stronger_arguments.description: (Carry from c2; promoted in c3 to address ISS-1965/Chebychev directly.) The empirical attack on the dome's Chebychev counter is to re-analyze the publicly-archived RAW (pre-Level-3) Membach 1999 SG record at minute-or-finer resolution and check whether the κ-predicted 3–9 µGal contact-phase pulse appears in the unfiltered data. If it does, the SG-null is a filtering artifact and the cross-eclipse vise weakens. If it does not — which is the expected outcome — the dome's Chebychev counter fails at empirical grounding, and the section can replace its rhetorical dismissal with a citation: 'we re-analyzed the publicly-archived raw Membach 1999 SG record at sub-minute resolution and found no κ-predicted pulse above 0.05 µGal; the Chebychev low-pass counter is refuted on its own terms.' This is a research opportunity worth commissioning before PRED-TIER-3's August 2026 window closes — it would close the empirical loop the section currently leaves open. The Membach 1999 raw SG record is publicly accessible via the Global Geodynamics Project archive.
sources: Global Geodynamics Project (GGP) data archive, http://www.eas.slu.edu/GGP/

stronger_arguments.description: (New in c3.) The dome's 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' naming (H9 above) suggests a principled scope-condition request: ask the dome to publish, in advance of August 2026 and in a timestamped venue independent of the mutable repository, the specific bedrock conductivity threshold above or below which κ couples. This is a binary test: either the dome publishes such a threshold pre-data and we can check whether 1999/2009 fall outside it (in which case κ is bedrock-conditional and our cross-eclipse vise weakens), or the dome cannot publish a pre-data threshold (in which case any post-eclipse bedrock-conditionality claim is exactly the kind of carve-out the OTS canary identifies). This is the section's strongest forward-looking ask and ties directly to the Timestamp Error tab's general lesson.
sources: data/sections.json:part4 (Timestamp Error tab — published independent-anchoring requirement)
Crossley & Hinderer 2010 on global SG bedrock conditions

kernel_of_truth.description: The structural argument is genuinely tight and has improved between c1 and c3 in citation hygiene at two specific points. The dome's κ = 1.677 nT/µGal IS a constant in their published material: it appears in the live-power dashboard's Domain 5 as a hardcoded predicted/observed pair (now correctly cited per ISS-1963 patch; the c1/c2 'Domain #7' error is closed), it is invoked across WIN-012/013/014/058b as the unifying coupling claim (data/wins.json:012,013,014,058b verified this c3), and PRED-TIER-3's 10–13 µGal target derivation is naturally read as ΔB/κ even if the dome's published predictions.json entry does not explicitly say so (PRED-TIER-3 verified this c3; data/predictions.json:PRED-TIER-3 reads 'SG contact-phase κ test, 10–13 µGal in Level-3 residuals' without derivation pathway — see ISS-1975). The cross-eclipse vise is the natural consequence of applying a constant constantly: if κ is universal, every eclipse with continuous SG sampling becomes a test, and the documented 1999 European Membach SG operation and 2009 East Asian Chinese SG network operation are independent test opportunities the dome's published material does not retroactively exempt (data/sections.json:part6 'What the dome could do' subsection, verified this c3). The first horn (1999/2009 nulls refute) is an honest empirical objection, and the second horn (scope conditions destroy PRED-TIER-3 derivation) is a real methodological constraint — published scope conditions must be independently timestamped to count (data/sections.json:part4 Timestamp Error tab).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being structurally elegant doesn't save the WIN-cluster from the dome's available counter-moves, and three cycles of curmudgeon review have surfaced nine citation/sourcing weaknesses that remain unfixed at c3. (a) The Chebychev low-pass argument (H1/ISS-1965) gives the dome a real methodological scope condition that the section currently dismisses without empirical engagement; 'post-hoc rescue' is rhetorical, not refutation (data/sections.json:part6 'What the dome could do' bundling, verified this c3). (b) The 2009 leg of the vise rests on ensemble-inferred ΔB (H3/ISS-1972) and an uncitable-able Sun et al. 2010 SG-null source (H4/ISS-1973) — empirically less firm than the 1999 leg (data/sections.json:part6 'What κ predicts during past eclipses' subsection, verified this c3). (c) PRED-TIER-3's 'ΔB/κ' derivation is OUR inference (H6/ISS-1975) and is paired with an equally-inferential 'no published mechanism' claim that needs joint reframing (new c3 H11). (d) Van Camp 2001's data-reduction methodology is uncharacterized (H5/ISS-1974); paired with the new temporal-resolution gap (new c3 H12), the section has two methodological gates on its SG-null citation that a defender can drive a wedge through. (e) The 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' naming (new c3 H9) is a published-prior scope hint that the section's dismissal underweights — co-opting the naming as a scope-condition request (per the new stronger_arguments item 3) is the principled response. (f) The cross-reference paragraph's '207 SG predictions' (new c3 H10) is an overstatement that a sophisticated defender would dismantle by intersecting the WIN-068 eclipse paths with the GGP SG network site map. (g) Three minor weaknesses (Hvoždara ResearchGate fragility per ISS-1976, the 30–600 factor-span selectivity per ISS-1977, and the new '(A note on units)' process artifact per c3 H13) and one new minor on internal Monte Carlo cross-referencing (new c3 H14) round out the citation hygiene problems. The section's reasoning is correct and the verdict (both horns lose at least one WIN/PRED) holds; its empirical foundation needs the c1/c2/c3 patches landed plus the new c3 reinforcements (Bedrock Induction co-option, 207 narrowing, joint H6/H11 reframe, temporal-resolution sentence, parenthetical deletion, Monte Carlo hedge).

text_fingerprint.verdict: structural — both horns lose at least one WIN/PRED

deeper_analysis:
c3 is the deep-attack singleton requested by queue_id 334 (class=deep-attack, target_type=section-rewrite). The c2 review under queue_id 333 closed at 2026-05-11T13:24:04Z with 11 holes (5 carry-forward from c1, 6 new in c2). Between c2 and c3 (separated by approximately 4 hours including queue ack and one decider run), TWO of the c1 carry-forwards have been patched (ISS-1963 Domain #7→#5; ISS-1967 1,000× → 50–500×) and NINE remain unfixed (ISS-1964/1965/1966 from c1; ISS-1972/1973/1974/1975/1976/1977 from c2). c3 brings six NEW findings: three moderate (Bedrock Induction Ratio naming co-option opportunity; 207 SG predictions overstatement; H6/H11 joint reframe; temporal resolution gap) and three minor (process-artifact parenthetical; internal Monte Carlo dependency). All new findings are non-verdict-changing. The recommended_action remains 'minor_edit'.

The most actionable c3 findings, ranked:

1. **New H9 (Bedrock Induction Ratio co-option)** is the highest-leverage finding — turns a dome-favorable naming into a scope-condition request that the dome must either honor in advance or concede the canary's point. This re-uses existing rhetorical infrastructure (Timestamp Error tab) and is a one-paragraph addition.

2. **Joint H6/H11 reframe (PRED-TIER-3 derivation + no-mechanism-claim)** is high-leverage because it patches two coupled sentences with consistent epistemic weight. The decider should treat ISS-1975 and new H11 as a single coupled patch when servicing the section.

3. **New H10 (207 SG predictions narrowing)** is medium-leverage but easy — replace 'each implying' with 'the subset where SGs were continuously sampling near totality' and the overstatement disappears. The full eclipse/SG intersection analysis is a follow-up research item, not blocking.

4. **New H12 (temporal resolution)** is the strongest empirically — adding one sentence on minute-binning amplitude dilution closes the most-likely follow-up defender objection.

5. **New H13 ('(A note on units)' parenthetical)** is a pure cosmetic delete; close-out cost ~30 seconds.

6. **New H14 (internal Monte Carlo dependency)** is the weakest of the c3 findings; can be deferred unless WIN-058b is re-examined.

On the carry-forwards: nine holes over three cycles is a sustained citation-hygiene weakness. The c1 patches that DID land (ISS-1963/1967) were 3-character / one-line fixes that the decider could self-apply; the unfixed nine all require some research (find Boy & Lyard 2008 in literature; locate Sun et al. 2010 or substitute; characterize Van Camp 2001 methodology; resolve Crossley DOI/year; replace ResearchGate link; reframe PRED-TIER-3 derivation; recompute 30-900 factor span; find specific 2009 ΔB; engage Chebychev empirically). The decider should consider routing the citation-hygiene subset (ISS-1964/1966/1976/1977) to analyst for batched research, and the empirical-engagement subset (ISS-1965/1972/1973/1974) for a coherent rewrite of 'What past SG data actually shows' + 'What the dome could do' subsections. ISS-1975 paired with new c3 H11 is a self-apply candidate for the decider (joint reframe of two sentences).

Verdict: structural — both horns lose at least one WIN/PRED. Confidence 0.74 (down from c2's 0.78 because the carry-forward count is now 9 over 3 cycles).

Hvoždara & Prigancová (2002) — ResearchGate link, not a primary publication URL; figures (ΔX = −5 to −8 nT, ΔY = +12 to +15 nT) not primary-source verified this c3 (ISS-1976 carry-forward).
Curto et al. (2006, JGR Space Phys. 111 A03306) — AGU URL not fetched this c3.

summary_for_decider: c3 deep-attack singleton review of SEC-6.14-kappa-cluster (queue_id 334, class=deep-attack). Findings: (1) TWO c1 carry-forwards PATCHED between c2 and c3 — ISS-1963 (Domain #7→#5) and ISS-1967 (1,000×→50–500×) — both now closed-as-fixed in open-issues.json. Confirms patches landed; good. (2) NINE carry-forwards STILL UNFIXED at c3 — ISS-1964/1965/1966 (c1, three cycles outstanding); ISS-1972/1973/1974/1975/1976/1977 (c2, two cycles outstanding). All listed in unresolved_prior_cycle[] for M3 enforcement. (3) SIX NEW c3 findings: H9 'Bedrock Induction Ratio' co-option opportunity (highest leverage; turns dome naming into scope-condition request via Timestamp Error tab); H10 '207 SG predictions' overstates co-location (narrow to 'subset where SGs sampling near totality'); H11 joint reframe of H6/ISS-1975 PRED-TIER-3 derivation + 'no published mechanism' sentence (decider self-apply candidate, paired patch); H12 temporal-resolution gap on SG residual binning (one-sentence add about 1-min binning amplitude dilution); H13 '(A note on units)' parenthetical is a process artifact (cosmetic delete, 30s); H14 SEC-6.14's cross-reference to WIN-058b's r>0.96 Monte Carlo inherits an internal-only simulation (hedge cross-reference language). (4) RECOMMENDED ROUTING: route citation-hygiene subset (ISS-1964/1966/1976/1977) to analyst as a batched research EXP; route empirical-engagement subset (ISS-1965/1972/1973/1974 + new H12) as a coherent rewrite EXP of 'What past SG data actually shows' + 'What the dome could do' subsections (the temporal-resolution sentence and Chebychev engagement go together); self-apply ISS-1975 + new H11 as the joint sentence reframe; self-apply new H13 as a parenthetical delete; self-apply new H10 as a one-phrase narrowing; defer new H9 to analyst as a one-paragraph addition (it's a content addition, not a fix); defer new H14 to next WIN-058b cycle. (5) Verdict HOLDS at confidence 0.74 (down from c2's 0.78 because nine-over-three-cycles is now the standing citation-hygiene weakness). recommended_action=minor_edit. (6) Three stronger arguments suggested (carried from c2 plus new c3 Bedrock-Induction co-option): co-located 2017 ΔB/Δg from BOU+BFO eclipse path; raw Membach 1999 SG re-analysis as direct attack on Chebychev counter; ask dome to publish bedrock-conductivity threshold for κ in advance of August 2026 in independently-anchored venue. None blocks the section landing; all are forward-looking sharpening opportunities.
